div.head: HO–8. Telegram from the Ambassador in Honduras (Newbegin) to the Department of StateSource: Department of State, Central Files, 615.173/5–2059. Confidential; Priority. Sent to Managua as telegram 67.
div.p:
438. In two-hour session this afternoon first with President Villeda and then with President of CongressModesto Rodas Alvarado. and Foreign Minister,Andrés Alvarado Puerto. reviewed full situation reference ratification Honduran-Nicaraguan Accord.
Pointed out as previously failure to ratify has put Honduras in unfavorable international position and one where at least as far as outside opinion concerned Honduras could be accused of bad faith. Referred to Rubottom’s conversation with Foreign Relations sub-committee (Department telegram 365)Not printed; 615.17/5–1959. and increasing criticism of Honduran position in U.S. Suggested if any incident should arise resulting in Nicaraguan appeal OAS Honduran posture hardly favorable. Also pointed out that during Velasquez crisis both Department and I had done everything possible to assist Honduras and agreement with subsequent expulsion Velasquez was part of these efforts. I would undoubtedly be handicapped in future efforts to assist Honduras if no action taken.

Not withstanding these and other arguments Rodas Alvarado made following points: 1) impossible because of technical requirements for Congress to ratify Accord before scheduled closing tomorrow even were deputies willing to approve. 2) He convinced that if agreement were submitted to Congress at this time would definitely be rejected. He, Alvarado Puerto and the President have all talked with various deputies trying to convince them of necessity for Honduran ratification but have not been successful. In addition to strong emotional prejudice against agreement with Nicaragua, Rodas said that a majority of the deputies had advised Villeda against signing the agreement and hence did not feel any obligation when he
proceeded contrary their advice. Deputies felt that Nicaragua itself had been guilty of bad faith during whole Velasquez incident since she and Honduras were already bound by previous international agreements to prevent just that sort of activity.

I am satisfied that President and Alvarado Puerto and possibly Rodas Alvarado have made genuine effort to obtain favorable action. Believe that if Rodas Alvarado’s conviction that Accord would be rejected is correct, lack of action is preferable such outcome, which could only further complicate Nicaraguan-Honduran relations. Alvarado Puerto has assured me as emphatically as he can that not withstanding lack of action on ratification, Honduras would act as though bound by agreement and would under no circumstances permit use her territory anti-Nicaraguan movements. He will also request Davila give similar assurances to Department.

While Embassy distressed inability obtain ratification this time I consider inaction essentially reflection basic public opposition Somoza regime and not indicative any unwillingness observe existing nonintervention commitments.
